document.title: Haemovigilance — Adverse Donation and Transfusion Events Reference
document.issued_by: Australian Red Cross Lifeblood — Medical & Scientific

adverse_donor_reactions.description: Adverse reactions can occur during or after donation. Lifeblood staff are trained to identify, grade, and manage all reaction types.
adverse_donor_reactions.classification_system: ISBT (International Society of Blood Transfusion) grading, adapted for Australian context.

reactions.name: Vasovagal Reaction (VVR)
reactions.frequency: Approximately 2–5% of whole blood donations; lower for apheresis.
reactions.description: A neurally-mediated response causing sudden drop in blood pressure and heart rate. Triggered by anxiety, pain, sight of blood, or reduced blood volume.

grades.description: Mild: pallor, sweating, nausea without loss of consciousness.
Moderate: brief loss of consciousness < 60 seconds, rapid recovery.
Severe: prolonged loss of consciousness > 60 seconds or injury due to fall.

reactions.management: Elevate legs, ensure airway, apply cold compress, monitor vitals. Grade 3: call emergency services.

reactions.name: Haematoma
reactions.description: Bruising caused by blood leaking from the vein into surrounding tissue during or after venepuncture.

grades.description: Small bruise confined to needle site.
Moderate bruise extending beyond needle site.
Large haematoma causing significant pain or neurovascular compromise.

reactions.management: Apply firm pressure and cold pack. Grade 3: refer for medical assessment.

reactions.name: Nerve Injury
reactions.description: Needle contacts a nerve branch at the antecubital fossa, causing sharp radiating pain, numbness, or tingling in the arm or hand.
reactions.management: Remove needle immediately if nerve contact suspected. Refer to GP if symptoms persist beyond 24 hours.

reactions.name: Citrate Reaction (Apheresis Only)
reactions.frequency: Mild symptoms in up to 15% of apheresis donors.
reactions.description: Citrate anticoagulant used in apheresis temporarily chelates calcium ions in the blood, causing hypocalcaemia symptoms.
symptoms: Rarely: cardiac arrhythmia in severe cases
reactions.management: Reduce apheresis flow rate or temporarily pause. Oral calcium (e.g., Tums) may be offered. Severe cases: IV calcium gluconate.

reactions.name: Arterial Puncture
reactions.description: Accidental needle insertion into an artery rather than a vein. Identified by rapid, bright red blood flow and pulsatile pressure in tubing.
reactions.management: Remove needle immediately, apply firm pressure for minimum 10 minutes, monitor, refer for medical assessment.

adverse_transfusion_reactions.description: Transfusion reactions occur in the recipient after infusion of blood products. Lifeblood supports hospital blood bank haemovigilance reporting.

reactions.name: Febrile Non-Haemolytic Transfusion Reaction (FNHTR)
reactions.frequency: Common: up to 0.5% of red cell transfusions; higher with platelets (now reduced by leukodepletion).
reactions.description: Temperature rise ≥ 1°C during or within 4 hours of transfusion, not attributable to haemolysis or infection.
reactions.management: Stop transfusion, assess for haemolysis (send samples to blood bank). If FNHTR confirmed, can restart at reduced rate with antipyretic cover.

reactions.name: Acute Haemolytic Transfusion Reaction (AHTR)
reactions.frequency: Rare: approximately 1 in 76,000 transfusions. Almost always due to ABO incompatibility from clerical error.
reactions.management: STOP transfusion immediately. Maintain IV access, fluid resuscitation, send blood bank samples, activate hospital transfusion emergency protocol.

reactions.name: Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury (TRALI)
reactions.frequency: Approximately 1 in 5,000–10,000 transfusions; reduced significantly by male-only plasma strategy.
reactions.description: New onset acute lung injury within 6 hours of transfusion, characterised by hypoxia and bilateral pulmonary infiltrates, not due to circulatory overload.
reactions.management: Supportive care, oxygen, possible ICU. Report to Lifeblood for investigation of implicated unit/donor.

reactions.name: Transfusion-Associated Circulatory Overload (TACO)
reactions.frequency: Most common serious transfusion reaction: up to 1 in 100 in at-risk populations (elderly, cardiac/renal disease).
reactions.description: Acute pulmonary oedema due to fluid overload from transfusion, presenting with respiratory distress, hypertension, and raised jugular venous pressure.
reactions.management: Stop or slow transfusion, diuretics, upright positioning, oxygen.

reactions.name: Transfusion-Associated Graft-versus-Host Disease (TA-GvHD)
reactions.description: Donor T-lymphocytes in the blood product engraft in an immunocompromised recipient and attack host tissues. Prevented by irradiation of cellular components for at-risk patients.
reactions.prevention: Irradiation of red cells and platelets for immunocompromised patients, HLA-matched donors, or directed family donations.

reporting.haemovigilance_system: ARCBS (Australian Red Cross Blood Service) Haemovigilance Program
reporting.hospital_reporting_obligation: Hospitals are required to report all serious adverse transfusion reactions to Lifeblood and the TGA under the Therapeutic Goods Act 1989.
reporting.annual_haemovigilance_report: Published annually on the Lifeblood website; data contributed to ISBT international haemovigilance registry.
